title: Mutation and gene editing
summary: A mutation is a change in the base sequence of a gene, and this lesson builds the whole D1.3 picture from that single idea. We cover the types of gene (point) mutation — substitution, insertion and deletion — and trace each through transcription and translation to its consequence for the protein: silent (neutral), missense, nonsense, and the frameshift that insertions and deletions cause. We look at where mutations come from (spontaneous replication errors and mutagens such as radiation and chemicals) and stress their random, undirected nature; why only germ-line mutations are heritable; and why mutation is the ultimate source of the genetic variation that evolution acts on, with sickle-cell anaemia as the worked substitution example. Finally we set out CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing and its applications and ethics. Every worked example is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards Paper 2 marks — point by point, with error-carried-forward — so you can see how each creditable statement protects your score.
learningObjectives: Define gene mutation and distinguish the types of point mutation: substitution, insertion and deletion.

Trace a specific base change through transcription and translation to classify its effect on the protein as silent (neutral), missense or nonsense, and explain why insertions and deletions cause a frameshift.
Explain the causes of mutation — spontaneous replication errors and mutagens (radiation, chemicals) — and describe why mutation is random and undirected.
Distinguish somatic from germ-line mutations and explain why only germ-line mutations are heritable, and why mutation is the ultimate source of genetic variation and the raw material for evolution.
Describe the CRISPR-Cas9 mechanism and discuss the applications and ethics of gene editing.
Present an explanation so that each distinct valid point earns a mark, using error-carried-forward (ECF) to protect the answer.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Editing the Book of Life
simpleExplanation.summary: DNA is a written instruction manual for building proteins. A mutation is a permanent typo in that manual — sometimes harmless, sometimes serious. Gene-editing tools such as CRISPR act like a precise 'find and replace' that can locate a specific typo and change it.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a codon as a three-letter word in a recipe. A substitution swaps one letter of one word — 'BAT' becomes 'CAT'; the recipe might still make sense (silent), read as a different word (missense), or turn into a full stop that ends the sentence early (nonsense). An insertion or deletion is worse: remove one letter and every word after it re-groups into gibberish, because the reader still reads in threes — that is a frameshift. Gene editing is a magic pen that finds one specific word and rewrites just that word.
steps: A gene is a sequence of DNA bases; read in triplets, it specifies the amino acid sequence of a protein.
A mutation changes that base sequence — a single-base substitution, or an insertion or deletion of bases.

The changed sequence may leave the protein unchanged (silent), change one amino acid (missense), end it early (nonsense), or — for insertions/deletions — shift the reading frame and garble everything downstream.
CRISPR-Cas9 uses a guide RNA to find one target sequence and the Cas9 enzyme to cut it, so a faulty sequence can be corrected.

flashcards.back: A change in the base (nucleotide) sequence of a gene. It is a permanent, heritable change in the DNA if it occurs in a cell line that is passed on.
One base is replaced by another, changing a single codon. Consequence depends on the new codon: silent, missense or nonsense. It does NOT shift the reading frame.
One or more bases are added to or removed from the sequence. If the number is not a multiple of three, all codons downstream are re-grouped — a frameshift.
A base change that does not alter the amino acid coded for, so the protein is unchanged. Possible because the genetic code is degenerate — several codons specify the same amino acid.

A substitution that changes one codon so it codes for a DIFFERENT amino acid, altering one residue of the polypeptide. May change the protein's folding, structure and function. Example: sickle-cell anaemia.
A substitution that turns a codon into a STOP codon, terminating translation early and giving a shortened, usually non-functional protein.

The reading-frame shift caused by an insertion or deletion that is not a multiple of three. Every codon after the mutation changes, so most amino acids downstream are wrong — usually a non-functional protein.

An environmental agent that raises the mutation rate — e.g. ionising and UV radiation, and chemicals such as those in tobacco smoke. Mutagens increase the chance of a change; they do not choose which gene changes.

No. Mutations are random and spontaneous with respect to what the organism 'needs'. A mutagen or an environment can raise the rate, but not steer a change towards a useful outcome. Natural selection — not mutation — is the directional step.
Somatic mutations occur in body cells and affect only the individual — they are NOT passed to offspring. Germ-line mutations occur in gametes (or their precursors) and CAN be inherited.
Mutation is the ULTIMATE source of new alleles and therefore of genetic variation. It supplies the raw material on which natural selection acts, so without it there would be no evolution.

A single base substitution in the β-globin gene changes codon 6 from GAG to GUG, replacing glutamic acid with valine — a missense mutation. The altered haemoglobin (HbS) polymerises when deoxygenated, distorting red blood cells into a sickle shape.

A gene-editing tool with two parts: a guide RNA (gRNA) complementary to the target DNA, and the Cas9 nuclease that cuts DNA. The gRNA directs Cas9 to a specific sequence, where it makes a double-strand break.
A short RNA engineered to be complementary to the target sequence. It base-pairs with that sequence and so guides Cas9 to the exact locus — the 'postcode' that gives CRISPR its specificity.
A substitution changes ONE codon only and never shifts the frame. Only an insertion or deletion (not a multiple of three) causes a frameshift. Calling a substitution a frameshift loses the mark.
False. Many are silent (no effect), some are neutral, and a few are beneficial — the source of adaptive variation. Only some are harmful. State the range, not a blanket claim.

quickCheck.prompt: A single base in a gene is replaced by a different base. Which term correctly describes this, and does it shift the reading frame?
options: A frameshift mutation; yes, all downstream codons change
A substitution; no, only one codon is affected
A deletion; yes, one codon is lost
An insertion; no, the frame is preserved
quickCheck.answer: A substitution; no, only one codon is affected

quickCheck.prompt: A substitution changes the mRNA codon from GAA to GAG. Both code for glutamic acid. This is best described as:
options: A missense mutation, because the base changed
A nonsense mutation, because a codon changed
A silent (neutral) mutation, because the genetic code is degenerate
A frameshift mutation, because a base was altered
quickCheck.answer: A silent (neutral) mutation, because the genetic code is degenerate

quickCheck.prompt: A mutation arises in a skin cell of an adult. With respect to inheritance, this mutation:
options: Will be passed to all the person's offspring
Cannot be inherited, because it is somatic, not germ-line
Will be inherited only by daughters
Becomes heritable if it is caused by a mutagen
quickCheck.answer: Cannot be inherited, because it is somatic, not germ-line

quickCheck.prompt: A population is exposed to a mutagen. Which statement is correct?
options: The mutagen directs mutations towards alleles the population needs
The mutagen raises the mutation rate, but which base changes is random
The mutagen guarantees only beneficial mutations occur
The mutagen causes mutations only in germ-line cells
quickCheck.answer: The mutagen raises the mutation rate, but which base changes is random

quickCheck.prompt: A substitution changes an mRNA codon into UAA, a stop codon, midway through a gene. The most likely consequence is:
options: No change, because stop codons are always ignored
One amino acid is swapped for another (missense)
A shortened, usually non-functional protein (nonsense mutation)
Every downstream amino acid is different (frameshift)
quickCheck.answer: A shortened, usually non-functional protein (nonsense mutation)

faq.q: How can a mutation change the DNA but not change the protein?
faq.a: The genetic code is degenerate: most amino acids are specified by more than one codon, and codons for the same amino acid often differ only in the third base. So a substitution — especially at the third base — can produce a new codon that still codes for the same amino acid. The amino acid sequence, and therefore the protein, is unchanged. This is a silent, or neutral, mutation, and it is why not every DNA change has a consequence.

faq.q: Why is a frameshift usually more damaging than a substitution?
faq.a: A substitution alters at most one codon, so it changes at most one amino acid (or none, or introduces one stop). An insertion or deletion that is not a multiple of three shifts the reading frame: the ribosome still reads in triplets, but the grouping is different from that point on, so every codon downstream is re-read and most amino acids after the mutation are wrong. A premature stop codon is also likely to appear. The result is usually a completely non-functional protein — which is why frameshifts tend to be far more disruptive than a single substitution.

faq.q: If most mutations are harmful or neutral, how do they drive evolution?
faq.a: Mutation is the only source of genuinely new alleles — new genetic variation. It happens at random, and most changes are neutral or harmful, but occasionally one is beneficial in a given environment. Natural selection then increases the frequency of that beneficial allele over generations. So the two steps are separate: mutation supplies the raw material (undirected), and selection provides the direction. Without mutation there would be nothing new for selection to act on.

faq.q: Why is editing germ-line cells more controversial than editing somatic cells?
faq.a: Editing somatic (body) cells affects only the treated individual, so it is comparable to other medical therapies. Editing germ-line cells — gametes or early embryos — changes DNA that is passed to every future generation. That raises deeper concerns: the consent of people not yet born, unforeseen long-term effects, off-target edits that are also inherited, unequal access, and the prospect of non-therapeutic 'enhancement'. These are why many countries permit somatic gene-editing research while restricting heritable germ-line editing.

sections.content: The base sequence of a gene is the instruction for building a protein, but that sequence is not fixed — it can change, and a change in the base sequence of a gene is a mutation. This lesson follows a mutation from the DNA outwards: the types of point mutation, what each does to the protein, where mutations come from, why only some are heritable, and why — despite most being neutral or harmful — mutation is the raw material that makes evolution possible. Sickle-cell anaemia serves as our worked example of a single base change, and CRISPR-Cas9 shows how such changes can now be made deliberately. Because the explanation marks in this topic are awarded point by point, every worked example is set out the way our marking engine actually awards them.
What a gene mutation is

A gene mutation is a change in the base (nucleotide) sequence of a gene. It may be as small as a single base or involve several bases. Mutations arise in two broad ways: spontaneously, as rare mistakes made by DNA polymerase during replication that escape proofreading, and by induction, when a mutagen in the environment increases the error rate. Either way the change is permanent once it is copied into a new DNA strand, and if it occurs in a cell line that gives rise to daughter cells it is passed on to them. The key point for everything that follows is that the effect of a mutation is decided at the level of the codon: how the change alters the triplet code determines whether the protein changes at all, and if so, how.
Types of gene (point) mutation

Gene mutations are classified by what happens to the bases. In a substitution, one base is replaced by a different base — this changes a single codon and never shifts the reading frame. In an insertion, one or more bases are added; in a deletion, one or more bases are removed. Insertions and deletions matter far more than their size suggests, because the ribosome reads mRNA in fixed groups of three from a set starting point. Add or remove a number of bases that is not a multiple of three and every codon after the change is re-grouped — a frameshift.

items: **Substitution:** one base swapped for another; one codon changed; reading frame preserved. Effect is silent, missense or nonsense.
**Insertion:** one or more bases added. If not a multiple of three, causes a frameshift.
**Deletion:** one or more bases removed. If not a multiple of three, causes a frameshift.
**Reading frame:** the fixed triplet grouping of the code. Substitutions leave it intact; frameshifts destroy it from the mutation onward.

sections.content: Consequences at the protein level

A substitution has one of three outcomes, and which one occurs depends entirely on the new codon. A silent (neutral) mutation produces a codon that still codes for the same amino acid — possible because the genetic code is degenerate, so several codons specify the same amino acid — and the protein is unchanged. A missense mutation produces a codon for a different amino acid, changing one residue in the polypeptide; depending on where it sits and how different the new amino acid is, this may barely matter or may disrupt the protein's folding and function. A nonsense mutation produces a stop codon, ending translation early and giving a shortened, usually non-functional protein. Insertions and deletions that cause a frameshift usually devastate the protein: every amino acid downstream is wrong and a premature stop codon commonly appears, so the polypeptide is almost always non-functional.

items: **Silent / neutral:** new codon, same amino acid (degenerate code) → protein unchanged.
**Missense:** new codon, different amino acid → one residue changed; may alter folding and function.
**Nonsense:** new codon is a stop codon → translation ends early → truncated, usually non-functional protein.
**Frameshift (from insertion/deletion):** all downstream codons re-grouped → most amino acids wrong → almost always non-functional.

sections.question:
Use this section of the genetic code (mRNA codon → amino acid): GAG → Glu (glutamic acid); GUG → Val (valine); GAA → Glu (glutamic acid); UAG → Stop.

In the β-globin gene, codon 6 of the mRNA normally reads GAG. In sickle-cell anaemia a single base substitution changes the middle base A→U, so the codon becomes GUG.

(a) State the amino acid coded by the normal codon and by the mutated codon.
(b) Classify this mutation and justify your classification.
(c) A different substitution changes the same GAG codon to GAA. State and explain the effect on the protein.
(d) Outline how the codon 6 change leads to the sickling of red blood cells. [6]
sections.solution:
**(a) Reading the two codons.**
Normal codon GAG → glutamic acid (Glu). Mutated codon GUG → valine (Val). [A1]

**(b) Classifying the mutation.**
One base has been replaced by another (A→U), so it is a SUBSTITUTION. [1] It changes the codon to one that codes for a DIFFERENT amino acid (Glu → Val), so it is a MISSENSE mutation. [1] Only one codon is affected and the reading frame is unchanged — it is not a frameshift.

**(c) The GAG → GAA substitution.**
GAA also codes for glutamic acid, the SAME amino acid as the original GAG. [1] So the amino acid sequence — and therefore the protein — is unchanged: this is a SILENT (neutral) mutation, possible because the genetic code is degenerate. [1]

**(d) From base change to sickling.**
Replacing glutamic acid (hydrophilic, charged) with valine (hydrophobic) on the surface of each β-globin chain creates a 'sticky' hydrophobic patch. When this haemoglobin (HbS) is deoxygenated, the patches make the molecules associate into long fibres that polymerise inside the red blood cell, distorting it into the rigid sickle shape. [1]

*Notice how one base decides everything: A→U gives a disease-causing missense change, while A→A-position change to GAA gives no effect at all. That contrast is exactly what silent-versus-missense questions test.*

sections.question:
Use this section of the genetic code (mRNA codon → amino acid): AUG → Met; AAA → Lys; CCC → Pro; GGG → Gly; AAC → Asn; CCG → Pro.

A short mRNA reads 5'-AUG AAA CCC GGG-3'. Two different mutations are studied:
Mutation 1 — the first A of the second codon is deleted.
Mutation 2 — the first A of the second codon is substituted to G (so AAA → GAA).

(a) Give the amino acid sequence of the original mRNA.
(b) State the new codons and the effect of Mutation 1, and name this type of mutation.
(c) Explain why Mutation 2 affects at most one amino acid, whereas Mutation 1 affects many. [5]
sections.solution:
**(a) Original sequence.**
AUG AAA CCC GGG → Met – Lys – Pro – Gly. [A1]

**(b) Mutation 1 — deletion.**
Deleting the first A of the second codon removes one base, so the ribosome re-groups everything after the deletion: the sequence becomes AUG · AAC · CCG · GG… → Met – Asn – Pro – … [1] Every codon after the deletion is different, and the message runs out of frame (the final GG is an incomplete codon). This is a FRAMESHIFT mutation, caused by the deletion. [1]

**(c) Why the two differ.**
Mutation 2 is a substitution: it replaces one base with another and shifts nothing, so only the single codon it lies in can change — at most one amino acid is altered (or none, if the change is silent). [1] Mutation 1 is a deletion of one base, which is not a multiple of three, so the reading frame shifts and every codon downstream is re-read incorrectly, changing many amino acids and usually introducing a premature stop. [1]

*This is the classic 'substitution versus frameshift' discrimination: the number of bases changed matters less than whether the reading frame survives.*

sections.content: Causes: mutagens and the random nature of mutation

Most mutations are spontaneous — rare copying errors during replication. Their rate is raised by mutagens: ionising radiation (such as X-rays and gamma rays) and ultraviolet light, which damage DNA directly, and chemical mutagens such as those in tobacco smoke and certain industrial compounds, which react with bases and cause mispairing. It is essential to be precise about what mutagens do. A mutagen increases the probability that a mutation occurs; it does not decide which gene mutates or steer the change towards anything useful. In this sense mutation is random and undirected with respect to the organism's needs: an environment that would benefit from a particular allele does not make that allele more likely to arise. This is the single most important conceptual point in the topic, and the one examiners most often test.

items: **Spontaneous:** rare, uncorrected replication errors — the baseline source of mutation.
**Mutagens (induced):** radiation (ionising, UV) and chemicals raise the mutation rate.
**Random and undirected:** mutagens change the RATE, not the DIRECTION — a needed allele is not made more likely by need.
**Two-step logic:** mutation supplies undirected variation; natural selection provides the direction.

sections.content: Where mutations matter: somatic versus germ-line

Whether a mutation can be inherited depends on which cells it occurs in. A somatic mutation arises in a body cell; it is copied to that cell's descendants within the individual — and can, for example, contribute to cancer — but it cannot be passed to offspring. A germ-line mutation arises in a gamete or in the cells that produce gametes; it can be inherited, and if it is, it will be present in every cell of the offspring. Only germ-line mutations are heritable, and only they contribute to the variation on which evolution acts. This distinction also underpins the ethics of gene editing: changing somatic cells affects one person, whereas changing germ-line cells changes all future generations.
Mutation as the raw material for evolution

Sexual reproduction — meiosis and fertilisation — reshuffles existing alleles into new combinations, but it cannot create an allele that did not already exist. Mutation is the ultimate source of new alleles, and therefore of all genetic variation. Most mutations are neutral or harmful, but occasionally one is beneficial in a particular environment. Natural selection then raises the frequency of that beneficial allele over generations. So mutation and selection play distinct roles: mutation is the undirected source of new variation, and selection is the directional filter. Without mutation there would be no new variation for selection to act on, and evolution would have no raw material.
CRISPR-Cas9: editing a specific sequence

CRISPR-Cas9 lets scientists make a change at a chosen point in the genome — in effect, a deliberate mutation. It was discovered as a bacterial defence system against viruses and adapted for use in other cells. It has two working parts: a guide RNA (gRNA), a short RNA engineered to be complementary to the target DNA sequence, and Cas9, a nuclease enzyme that cuts DNA. The gRNA base-pairs with its complementary sequence in the genome, which brings Cas9 to precisely that locus; Cas9 then makes a double-strand break. The cell's own repair machinery seals the break — and if scientists also supply a corrected DNA template, the cell can copy it during repair, changing the sequence to the desired version.

items: **Design:** a gRNA is made complementary to the target gene sequence.
**Target:** the gRNA base-pairs with the matching genomic sequence, giving the specificity.
**Cut:** Cas9, guided to that locus, makes a double-strand break in the DNA.
**Repair:** the cell repairs the break; a supplied correct template can be copied in, editing the sequence.

sections.content: To earn CRISPR marks you must name BOTH components and their roles: the guide RNA gives specificity by base-pairing with the target, and Cas9 is the enzyme that cuts. 'CRISPR cuts the DNA' alone is not enough — say what targets the cut and what makes it.
Applications and ethics

Gene editing has broad potential: correcting the single-gene mutations behind disorders such as sickle-cell anaemia and cystic fibrosis, engineering crops for higher yield or disease resistance, and creating precise research models. But the same power raises hard questions. The central ethical line is between somatic editing (affecting only the treated individual, and broadly comparable to other therapies) and germ-line editing (heritable, affecting all future generations). Further concerns include off-target edits, unequal access, consent for people not yet born, and the difference between treating disease and non-therapeutic 'enhancement'. These are why heritable germ-line editing in humans is widely restricted even where somatic gene-editing research is permitted.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Calling a substitution a frameshift** — a substitution changes only one codon and never shifts the reading frame. Only insertions or deletions (not a multiple of three) cause a frameshift.
**Forgetting silent mutations** — because the code is degenerate, a base change can leave the amino acid unchanged. An answer that assumes every substitution changes the protein is incomplete.
**Saying 'all mutations are harmful'** — many are neutral or silent and a few are beneficial. State the full range, and note that beneficial mutations are the source of adaptive variation.
**Claiming somatic mutations are inherited** — only germ-line mutations (in gametes or their precursors) can be passed to offspring. Somatic mutations affect only the individual.
**Saying mutations are 'directed' by need or by a mutagen** — mutations are random; a mutagen raises the rate, not the direction. Natural selection, not mutation, supplies the direction.
**Describing CRISPR as just 'cutting DNA'** — you must state that the guide RNA provides specificity by complementary base-pairing and that Cas9 is the enzyme that cuts.
**Confusing missense and nonsense** — missense = different amino acid; nonsense = premature stop codon and a truncated protein.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

This is an 'explain' question, so the marks are awarded analytically: each distinct valid biological point is worth one mark, up to the maximum. Method-style points (M) credit correct reasoning about the mechanism, answer points (A) credit a correct stated outcome, and error-carried-forward (ECF) means that if you set up one step wrongly but reason correctly from it, the later points can still be earned. Study how each mark below is tied to a specific named idea, not to loose phrasing.

sections.question: Explain how a single base substitution in a gene may or may not affect the protein produced. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
A substitution changes one base and therefore alters a single codon of the mRNA. Because the genetic code is degenerate, the new codon may still code for the same amino acid, so the amino acid sequence — and hence the protein — is unchanged; this is a silent (neutral) mutation. Alternatively, the new codon may code for a different amino acid (a missense mutation), changing one residue of the polypeptide and possibly altering the protein's folding, structure and function. Or the new codon may become a stop codon (a nonsense mutation), which ends translation early and produces a shortened, usually non-functional protein. Which outcome occurs depends entirely on the new codon.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each distinct valid point scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **Point 1 (M):** a substitution changes one base / alters a single codon (reading frame unchanged).
- **Point 2 (A):** because the code is DEGENERATE the new codon may code for the SAME amino acid → silent/no effect on the protein.
- **Point 3 (A):** the new codon may code for a DIFFERENT amino acid → missense → one residue changed, altering the protein.
- **Point 4 (A):** the new codon may become a STOP codon → nonsense → translation ends early → truncated / non-functional protein.

The engine credits DISTINCT ideas: naming both 'silent' and 'no change to the protein' is one point, not two. Because the question is worth 4 and there are several creditable outcomes, there is deliberate room to miss one and still score full marks.

**How A marks and ECF work here.** The A marks are for the correct OUTCOME attached to each case (silent → unchanged; missense → altered; nonsense → truncated). Equivalent wording is accepted — 'neutral' for silent, 'premature stop codon' for nonsense, 'polypeptide' for protein. Under ECF, a student who defines degeneracy slightly wrongly but then reasons correctly to 'same amino acid, no change' still earns that outcome mark, because the reasoning chain is intact.

**What the engine rejects.** 'A substitution always changes the protein' scores no more than the mechanism point, because it misses the silent case. 'The mutation is bad for the cell' scores nothing — it names no codon-level mechanism and no specific outcome.

**Bottom line:** to secure all 4 marks, state the single-codon change, then give the three possible outcomes tied to their causes (degeneracy → silent; different amino acid → missense; stop codon → nonsense). Each named case is a separate mark.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — point by point, with ECF and a cap at the marks available. So write in distinct, creditable statements: name the single-codon change, then each outcome with its cause (degeneracy → silent, different amino acid → missense, stop codon → nonsense). Vague statements like 'it might change the protein' earn nothing; the named mechanism and outcomes are what protect your score.
Where this leads

Mutation ties this topic to the rest of the course. It is the source of the new alleles that population genetics and natural selection then act on; the substitution/missense/nonsense logic depends directly on transcription, translation and the degenerate genetic code; and CRISPR-Cas9 is applied molecular biology built on complementary base-pairing and enzyme specificity. Master the codon-level reasoning here — trace a base change to its codon, then to the amino acid, then to the protein — and both the exam calculations and the bigger evolutionary picture fall into place.
